sections.label: USER PROMPT
sections.content:
The code executed 2 actions in the previous sequence.
Executed actions: MOUSE(row=33, col=44), MOUSE(row=55, col=20).
You are still on the same level.
Current state: step 179, level 2.
Valid actions right now: MOUSE.
Only tool: `python`. It receives `current_frame`, `previous_frame`, `history`, `transitions`, `last_transition`, `valid_actions`, `last_action_result`, and `action(actions)`. It also provides `last_animation` (per-action frames of your last `action(...)` call) and `frame_stats` (how much this game has animated so far) -- optional; use them only if you judge the intermediate motion informative rather than incidental.
Only letter-coded board views and lightweight metadata are exposed; raw numeric color IDs are not available.
Keep tool output compact: use `current_frame.segmentation` as the primary view, and `current_frame.ascii` only for a small specific region; never print full boards.
For the most recent change, compare `previous_frame` to `current_frame`, or `last_transition.before_frame` to `last_transition.after_frame`; `history[-1].frame` is the current frame, not the previous one.
Use Python to inspect the evidence, refine that world model from the newest history, and search or score candidate actions or short sequences against the current goal as you currently understand it.
Maintain a compact working world model of what the current level seems to contain, what actions appear to do, what the goal seems to be, what is still uncertain, and what plan currently looks best.
Below you are provided with the current world model from the previous turn. The default behavior is to copy it and add or remove things based on the evidence that you gathered. BEFORE EXECUTING NEW ACTIONS YOU MUST ALWAYS GIVE THE REVISED VERSION OF THE WORLD MODEL.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it, but stop immediately if a result reports `game_over`, `run_complete`, `level_completed`, or `done`.
Working world model carried from earlier turns:
- World model: Number of active columns = number of movement steps. Row 1=LEFT, Row 2=RIGHT, Row 3=nothing. Shape 2 always moves, shape 1 never moves with these rows. Need to find rows that control shape 1 and vertical movement. New hypothesis: Maybe the left panel selector (blue square at bottom) controls which shape is active. Let me try clicking it.
- Plan: Click the left panel selector to see if it changes which shape the bars control.
- Revise any item above immediately if `current_frame` or `history` contradicts it.
end of world model. 
Focus on what changed most recently in `history`, update the target environment change if needed, and separate gameplay-object changes from HUD-only changes.
When ready, call `action(actions)` from inside the `python` tool with the best valid action or ordered batch selected by your code. If your code has found a reliable short sequence, prefer batching it in one call.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it.
If you include assistant text before a tool call, keep it short and use it to update the world model. Helpful optional prefixes are `World model:`, `Goal model:`, `Action model:`, `Recent findings:`, `Open questions:`, `Plan:`, and `Cross-level notes:`.
When calling `python`, emit exactly the tool-call format shown elsewhere in this prompt for this model. Use only that format; do not add markdown fences, prose wrappers, or alternate tool-call syntax. Do not quote or place tool-call markup inside explanatory text; when you decide to call the tool, emit the tool call itself.
If you use MOUSE, include integer row and col arguments.
EFFICIENCY BUDGET — your score on each level is (baseline_actions / your_actions)^2, so every wasted action costs you quadratically.
Level 2: you have used 158 actions; a strong score needs about 72 or fewer. You are 2.2x over the target.
NET-ZERO WASTE: your last 62 actions returned the board to a state already seen 62 actions ago — that round-trip made no progress yet still cost 62 actions. Do not extend-and-retract or oscillate; a sequence that ends where it began is pure waste.
If you are not steadily making progress toward the level goal, commit to your single best hypothesis and the shortest sequence that tests it — do not exhaustively scan rows/columns or enumerate every option. Test one idea, read the result, then decide.

sections.label: THINKING
sections.content:
Great! Clicking the left panel selector changed things:
1. The left panel bars changed: now rows 1 AND 6 are all active (B B B B), while rows 2-5 are all inactive (w w w w).
2. The left panel shape changed from a "C" to what looks like an "H" or two vertical bars.
3. The selectors at the bottom swapped - now the right panel selector (with the "C" shape) is highlighted in blue.
This is a major discovery! The selectors control which panel is "active" and show different target patterns. When I clicked the left selector:
- Left panel now shows rows 1 and 6 active
- The shape in the left panel changed
- The right selector is now highlighted
So there are multiple "modes" or "targets" to achieve. The game might require me to match multiple bar patterns.
Let me think about this:
- Mode 1 (left selector active): left panel shows row 1 all active, target is "C" shape
- Mode 2 (right selector active): left panel shows rows 1 and 6 all active, target is "H" shape
Maybe I need to set the right panel bars to match the left panel bars for each mode, and then click the blue circle to apply the movement.
But wait, when I tried matching row 1 all active in mode 1, the shapes moved but returned. The level didn't complete.
Now in mode 2, the left panel shows rows 1 AND 6 all active. Maybe I need to set the right panel bars to match this pattern (rows 1 and 6 all active).
Let me try this. First, I need to set the right panel bars to match:
- Row 1: B B B B
- Row 6: B B B B
- Rows 2-5: w w w w
Then click the blue circle to see if the level completes.
But I'm concerned about wasting actions. Let me think about what the left panel bars represent:
- Row 1: LEFT movement
- Row 6: ? (haven't tested yet)
If row 6 controls a different direction (maybe UP or DOWN), then setting both rows 1 and 6 active would move the shapes in two directions.
Let me try setting the right panel bars to match the left panel pattern and see what happens.

sections.label: ASSISTANT
sections.content:
World model: Clicking left panel selector changed the bar pattern! Left panel now shows rows 1 AND 6 all active. The selectors swap which panel is "active". This reveals multiple target patterns.
Key insight: Need to match right panel bars to left panel bars for each mode. Current mode shows rows 1 and 6 active.
Plan: Set right panel bars to match left panel (rows 1 and 6 all B), click blue circle to test.

sections.label: TOOL CALL: python
sections.content:
<tool_call>
<function=python>
<parameter=code>
# Set right panel bars to match left panel: rows 1 and 6 all B
# Row 1: cols 39, 44, 49, 54
# Row 6: cols 39, 44, 49, 54 (row 6 is at display row 48)
set_bars = [
    {'action': 'MOUSE', 'row': 33, 'col': c} for c in [39, 44, 49, 54]  # row 1
] + [
    {'action': 'MOUSE', 'row': 48, 'col': c} for c in [39, 44, 49, 54]  # row 6
]

action(set_bars + [{'action': 'MOUSE', 'row': 55, 'col': 46}])

# Check result
print(f"Level completed: {last_action_result.get('level_completed', False)}")
print(f"Done: {last_action_result.get('done', False)}")

# Check animation
if last_animation:
    for entry in last_animation:
        if len(entry.frames) > 1:
            print(f"\nBlue circle: {len(entry.frames)} frames")
            for j, frame in enumerate(entry.frames):
                seg = frame.segmentation
                y_shapes = [n for n in seg['nodes'] if n['color'] == 'Y' and n['pixels'] > 10]
                if y_shapes:
                    centers = [(round(sum(p[0] for p in n['boundary'])/len(n['boundary']), 1), 
                               round(sum(p[1] for p in n['boundary'])/len(n['boundary']), 1)) for n in y_shapes]
                    print(f"  Frame {j}: {centers}")
</parameter>
</function>
</tool_call>
